issue: Hamburger menu button is missing `aria-expanded` attribute to communicate whether the mobile menu is currently open or closed.
detail: The button toggles `menuOpen` state but does not reflect this via `aria-expanded={menuOpen}`. Screen reader users cannot determine the current state of the collapsible menu.
recommendation: Add `aria-expanded={menuOpen}` to the hamburger button element.

issue: Hamburger button is missing `aria-controls` to associate it with the mobile menu panel it toggles.
detail: Without `aria-controls`, assistive technologies cannot programmatically determine which region of the page is controlled by this button.
recommendation: Add an `id` to the mobile menu `<div>` (e.g., `id="mobile-menu-panel"`) and set `aria-controls="mobile-menu-panel"` on the hamburger button.

issue: The `aria-label` is static ("메뉴 열기" / "Open menu") and does not change when the menu is open. When the menu is expanded, the label should reflect "메뉴 닫기" (Close menu).
detail: The SVG icon changes between hamburger and X visually, but the accessible name stays the same regardless of state.
recommendation: Use a dynamic aria-label: `aria-label={menuOpen ? '메뉴 닫기' : '메뉴 열기'}`.

issue: The mobile menu container `<div>` lacks `role="menu"` or `role="navigation"` and has no `aria-label` to identify it as a navigation region.
detail: The mobile menu is rendered conditionally but is a plain `<div>` with no landmark role. Assistive technology users cannot easily locate or identify this region.
recommendation: Either wrap mobile links in a `<nav aria-label="모바일 메뉴">` or add `role="navigation"` with an `aria-label`.

issue: Desktop navigation links are wrapped in a generic `<div>` instead of a semantic `<ul>/<li>` list structure or a nested `<nav>`.
detail: Using a list structure for navigation links allows screen readers to announce the number of items and provides better landmark navigation. Alternatively, the links could be grouped inside a labeled region.
recommendation: Wrap the navigation links in `<ul>` with each link in an `<li>`, or use a nested landmark structure.

issue: The `<nav>` element lacks an `aria-label` to distinguish it from other navigation landmarks on the page (e.g., breadcrumbs could also be a `<nav>`).
detail: When a page has multiple `<nav>` elements, each should have a unique accessible name so users can differentiate them.
recommendation: Add `aria-label="메인 내비게이션"` to the primary `<nav>` element.

issue: Breadcrumbs are rendered inside a `<div>` instead of a `<nav>` with `aria-label="breadcrumb"`. The breadcrumb items also lack an `<ol>` list structure.
detail: The WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices recommend breadcrumbs be placed in a `<nav aria-label="breadcrumb">` containing an `<ol>` list. The current item should have `aria-current="page"`.
recommendation: Replace the outer `<div>` with `<nav aria-label="breadcrumb">`, wrap items in `<ol>/<li>`, and add `aria-current="page"` to the last breadcrumb.

issue: The currently active navigation link (determined by `pathname === link.href`) has no `aria-current="page"` attribute.
detail: While the active link is visually distinguished with a different color, screen reader users have no programmatic indication of which link corresponds to the current page.
recommendation: Add `aria-current={pathname === link.href ? 'page' : undefined}` to each nav link.

issue: The SVG icon inside the hamburger button lacks `aria-hidden="true"` to ensure it is ignored by assistive technology.
detail: Although the parent button has `aria-label`, the SVG itself is not explicitly hidden. Some screen readers may attempt to announce the SVG content.

issue: Filter toggle buttons are missing `aria-pressed` to indicate the currently selected filter state.
detail: The buttons visually distinguish the active filter via background color, but screen reader users cannot determine which filter is currently active. The code comment on line 30 explicitly acknowledges this issue (A-33).
recommendation: Add `aria-pressed={filter === f.value}` to each filter button.

issue: The search input lacks an associated `<label>` element or `aria-label` attribute. Only `placeholder` is provided, which is not a reliable accessible name.
detail: Placeholder text disappears when the user types and is not consistently announced by all screen readers as the accessible name for the input.
wcag: WCAG 2.1 SC 1.3.1 (Info and Relationships), SC 3.3.2 (Labels or Instructions)
recommendation: Add `aria-label="할 일 검색"` to the input, or provide a visible or visually-hidden `<label>` element associated via `htmlFor`.

issue: The SearchFilter component's root element is a generic `<div>`. A `<search>` element (HTML5) or `role="search"` landmark would better convey the purpose of this region.
detail: Using a `<search>` element or `role="search"` allows screen reader users to jump directly to the search functionality via landmark navigation.
recommendation: Replace the outer `<div>` with `<search>` (if targeting modern browsers) or add `role="search"` to the wrapper.

issue: The filter button group wrapper is a plain `<div>` instead of using `role="group"` with an `aria-label` to convey that the buttons form a related set.
detail: Without grouping semantics, screen readers announce each button independently with no context that they form a filter control set.
recommendation: Add `role="group"` and `aria-label="필터"` to the wrapper `<div>`, or use a `<fieldset>/<legend>` pattern.
